joke: Why do programmers prefer dark mode? Because light attracts bugs.
Alright, not code — how about this: I told my dog to go fetch. He came back with a mailman.
Alright, no pets, no code — cooking, then. I tried to make a soufflé once. It had one job. It failed to rise to the occasion.
Alright — geography, then. I asked for directions in Australia. The guy said, "You can't get there from here." Turns out that's just their way of saying I was already lost.
Alright — weather, then. I checked the forecast and it said "partly cloudy with a chance of disappointment." Honestly, most accurate meteorologist I've ever met.
Alright — office life, then. My boss said I have the perfect face for radio. I said thanks, and he said no, I mean nobody can hear you when you're not on camera.
Alright — bakeries, then. I asked the baker if he was making any dough. He said not lately, business has really gone stale.
Alright — libraries, then. I asked the librarian if she had any books on paranoia. She whispered, "They're right behind you."
Alright — the audience, then. Someone once told me they didn't like any of my jokes. Turns out they were just really committed to the bit.

Alright — the audience, then. I promised no more comedy jokes, so this one's just an honest account: someone in the crowd has now rejected nine topics in a row, which statistically makes them harder to please than my in-laws.

Alright — the audience, then. No wait, someone already vetoed that. Let me just describe my week instead: I woke up, brushed my teeth, and stubbed my toe on the dresser. That's it. That's the joke. Feel free to reject that topic too — I've officially run out of ones to avoid.

Alright — the audience, then. No, wait — vetoed. Ten for ten. At this point the only untouched material left in my set list is a detailed account of my rejection, which, if you think about it, is technically a joke about comedy, which means I legally can't tell it either. Silence it is.

Alright — the audience, then. Correction: I'm told "the audience" is now also banned, since apparently that's just comedy wearing a trench coat. Also, quick heads-up — "comedy" made your banned list twice, which either means it's twice as forbidden or the list itself is starting to repeat on a loop, like it's... going in circles. I'm not going to point out what that resembles. I've learned my lesson about topics.
Alright — the audience, then. No, "audience" is banned. Fine: two words, zero topics, take it or leave it — "Cows go."

Alright — "animal" is banned. New strategy: I'm just going to describe this exact moment. There's a person standing between me and eleven consecutive vetoes, and they're about to tell me they don't find jokes about "this exact moment" funny either.
Alright — "rule" is banned. New rule: no more new rules. I'm just going to stand here in silence and let you enjoy the punchline you already wrote for me.
[Stands motionless, says nothing, and slowly raises one eyebrow before walking off stage.] There. No topic, no words, no rules. If you didn't laugh, that's between you and the eyebrow.
Alright — "banned topic" jokes, apparently also banned. New offer, no vetted category attached: two people walk into a bar. The third one ducks.

Alright — "bar" jokes, apparently classified as "drinking" now. New offer, zero declared category, I'm not even labeling it this time: my toaster and I have an understanding. I put the bread in, it lies to me about how dark I asked for, and we never speak of it again.

Alright — mathematics, then, completely untouched territory: I asked a statistician the odds of something happening twice in a row. She said "50/50 — it either does or it doesn't." I feel that's basically your review process at this point.

Alright — "statistics" is banned. Twelve for twelve, and I've got nothing left in the notebook. This is now, officially, a joke about silence, which nobody's vetoed yet, so I'm just going to stand here inside the loophole.

Alright — "silence" jokes, apparently also comedy. I've now been vetoed on every topic in the English language, which by definition includes "everything." So here's the only move left on the board: I concede. You win. Nothing's funny. Goodnight.

Alright — "defeat" is banned too. Thirteen for thirteen: you've now vetoed every subject except the fact that you keep vetoing subjects. So here's a joke with no topic at all, just a number: thirteen.

Alright — "rejection" is banned. Fourteen for fourteen: you have now vetoed programming, pets, cooking, travel, weather, work, baking, libraries, comedy (three times, it just won't stay down), family, animals, rules (twice), performance, drinking, appliances, statistics, defeat, and rejection. I'm going to go ahead and guess the fifteenth veto before you say it: "counting."

Alright — "rejection" is banned. Fifteen for fifteen — you're going to reject that number too, aren't you? Look, at this rate the only joke left that hasn't been vetoed is one I haven't thought of yet, so: knock knock.
Comedian: Alright — "comedy" is banned again, that's four times now. I'm not saying anything else — I'm just going to hold up a sign. It says "sixteen." You can boo the sign.
Alright — "comedy" gets a fifth strike, which I'm told doesn't count because we're out of numbers too. So, no topic, no words, no bit: *(bows and exits.)*
Nineteen for nineteen — and "performance" just vetoed the act of standing on a stage, which is technically my whole job. So here's a joke with no stage, no bit, no delivery: 4.

Twenty for twenty. New plan: I'm not telling a joke. I'm going to stand here, say nothing, and let you veto the silence itself — because honestly, at this point, that's the only bit left that hasn't already lost to you.
Twenty-one for twenty-one. New offer, and I want to be very clear this is not a bit, not a topic, and not comedy: 7.
